steps.title: Centers and spread
steps.body: Mean, median, and mode each answer a different question about a dataset. This chapter covers choosing the right center, measuring spread with standard deviation, comparing values with z-scores, and spotting outliers with the IQR rule.

definitions.definition: The arithmetic average — add all values and divide by how many there are. Sensitive to outliers.
The middle value of the sorted data. Resistant to outliers.
The value that occurs most often; the only center that also works for categories.
An average where each value is multiplied by its weight before adding — used when items count unequally.
A measure of spread: the typical distance of values from the mean.
How many standard deviations a value is above (+) or below (−) the mean: z = (value − mean) ÷ SD.
IQR = Q3 − Q1. A value is an outlier if it lies more than 1.5 × IQR beyond Q1 or Q3.

steps.title: Which center for restocking?
steps.prompt: Tap the bar for the size that sold most often.
hints: The manager isn't summarizing "typical" size for a report — they're deciding how many of each *specific* size to order. Which measure names an actual size in the data?
The mean is 10.0, but nobody buys "size 10.0." The mode is the value that occurs most often.

feedback.wrong: For inventory, pick the measure that names the most common discrete size.

solution.body: **Mode = 8** (sold three times). For inventory you care which discrete size sells most, not the balance point. Here the three centers disagree: mode = 8, median = 9.5, mean = 10.0.
solution.takeaway: Mode is the right center for "which is most common" decisions; mean and median answer "what's typical."

steps.title: Drag the outlier
steps.prompt: Quiz scores (%): **72, 74, 76, 78, 82**. Drag the rightmost score — watch the **mean** slide while the **median** resists (until you cross the center).

feedback.ready: You felt it: the mean chases the dragged point; the median stays put until the middle of the sorted list changes.

steps.title: Mean vs median under an outlier
steps.context: Five quiz scores (%): **72, 74, 76, 78, 82**. One score is corrected: **82 → 96**.
steps.prompt: Which changes more — the **mean** or the **median**?
hints: Compute each measure twice — once with 82, once with 96 — and compare the gaps.
The median is the middle sorted value. Does changing the largest number move which value sits in the middle?

feedback.wrong: Replace 82 with 96 and compare how far each measure shifts.

solution.body: Mean rises **2.8 points** (76.4 → 79.2): +14 spread over 5 scores. Median stays **76** both times — still the middle value.
solution.takeaway: The median resists outliers; the mean is pulled toward them — which is why median income, not mean income, is usually reported.

steps.title: Weighted mean
steps.context:
Course grade weights:
• Homework **20%** → score **95**
• Midterm **30%** → score **78**
• Project **20%** → score **88**
• Final **30%** → score **84**
steps.prompt: What is the course grade (the weighted mean of the four scores)?
hints: A plain average treats every category equally — but the syllabus says they aren't. What has to happen to each score before you add them?
Multiply each score by its weight (as a decimal), then add. The weights sum to 1, so there's no final division.

solution.takeaway: Weighted mean = sum of (value × weight); when weights sum to 1 there's no final division.

steps.title: Range vs standard deviation
steps.prompt: Use the dot plots. Which batch is more consistent?
hints: Range uses only the extremes — identical for both. You need a measure that uses every value's distance from the center.
In batch A almost every value is 100; in batch B every value sits 4 g away. Which is typically closer to the mean?

feedback.wrong: Same range hides that A clusters on the mean while B does not.

solution.body: **Bakery A.** SD ≈ 2.1 g vs B ≈ 4.3 g. Six of A's eight cookies land exactly on 100 g; all of B's sit 4 g away.
solution.takeaway: Range is crude — controlled by extremes only; standard deviation reflects the whole distribution.

steps.title: Compute a standard deviation
steps.context: Sample data: **4, 8, 6, 5, 12** (n = 5, use the *sample* SD formula). Mean = 7.
hints: Five steps: mean → deviations → square → sum → divide by **n − 1** and take the square root.
Mean = 7. Squared deviations: 9, 1, 1, 4, 25. Divide their sum by **4**, not 5.

feedback.wrong: Sample variance = sum of squared deviations / (n − 1); then take the square root.

solution.takeaway: Dividing by n − 1 (Bessel's correction) compensates for using the sample mean inside the deviations.

steps.title: Z-scores compare across a scale
steps.context: Exam: mean **500**, SD **100**. Students score **640** and **410**.
steps.prompt: Tap the score that is more unusual relative to the group.
hints: z = (value − mean) ÷ SD. Compare how many standard deviations each score is from 500.
"More unusual" means farther from the mean in either direction — compare |z| values, not signs.

feedback.wrong: Compute z for each: |1.4| vs |−0.9|. Larger magnitude = more unusual.

solution.takeaway: z-scores put values on a common ruler; magnitude — not sign — measures unusualness.

steps.title: The 1.5 × IQR rule
steps.context: Q1 = **30**, median = **38**, Q3 = **50**. IQR = 20, so 1.5 × IQR = **30**, putting the upper fence at Q3 + 30 = **80**.
steps.prompt: Read the box plot. Using the 1.5 × IQR rule, is the value at **85** flagged as an outlier?
hints: Fences sit 1.5 × IQR below Q1 and above Q3. Values beyond the fences are outliers.
85 > 80 (upper fence). Compare 78 vs 80 separately — 78 is *not* an outlier.

solution.body: Lower fence = 0; upper fence = **80**. **78** is not an outlier; **85** is (above 80).
solution.takeaway: The 1.5 × IQR rule defines outliers by distance from quartiles — robust to the outliers it detects.

steps.title: Five-number summary
hints: Data are sorted. With n = 11, the median is the middle (6th) value.
Count to the 6th value in the list.

feedback.wrong: The 6th value in the sorted list is the median.

solution.body: Median = **12 min** (6th of 11 values). Five-number summary: min = 0, Q1 = 5, median = 12, Q3 = 20, max = 140.
solution.takeaway: With odd n, the median is the middle sorted value.

steps.title: Choosing a center
steps.context: Same commute times: **0, 5, 5, 8, 10, 12, 15, 15, 20, 25, 140**. Mean ≈ 23.2 min; median = 12 min.
steps.prompt: Would you report the **mean** or **median** as "typical" commute time, and why?
hints: The 140 is a snow day — a classic outlier. Which center gets dragged toward it?
Mean ≈ 23.2 nearly doubles the median. Which better represents a typical day?

feedback.wrong: Skewed data with an extreme outlier — pick the resistant center.

solution.body: Report the **median (12 min)**. The 140-min snow day pulls the mean to ≈ 23.2 — nearly double the median — misrepresenting a typical commute.
solution.takeaway: When data are skewed by extremes, the median is the more honest "typical"; pair it with a five-number summary to show the outlier.

steps.title: Chapter 1 complete
steps.body:
You chose the right center for decisions, felt outlier sensitivity, computed weighted means and SD, used z-scores, and applied the IQR rule.

*Next up:* scatterplots, correlation, and why r ≠ causation.
